hints: Imperative sentences start with the base form of the verb (infinitive without 'to'), not the -s or -ing form.
The object 'the door' after the gap signals a direct command.

a1.Open: Correct: 'Open' is the base form used in imperative sentences to give a direct command.
a1.Opens: Wrong: 'Opens' is the third-person singular form (he/she opens). Imperatives use the base form without -s.
a1.Opening: Wrong: 'Opening' is the -ing form. Imperatives require the base form of the verb.

hints: Imperatives use the base form: sit, not sits or sitting.
The word 'down' after the gap forms the phrasal verb 'sit down' — a common classroom command.

a2.Sit: Correct: 'Sit' is the base form for the imperative. 'Sit down' is a typical command.
a2.Sits: Wrong: 'Sits' has the third-person -s ending. Imperatives never use -s.
a2.Sitting: Wrong: 'Sitting' is the -ing form, which cannot begin an imperative sentence.

hints: The base form of the verb is used for commands. No -s or -ing ending is needed.
After the gap, 'the window' is the object — someone is being told to close the window.

a3.Close: Correct: 'Close' is the base form for a direct command.
a3.Closes: Wrong: 'Closes' has the -s ending for third person. Imperatives use the base form.
a3.Closing: Wrong: 'Closing' is the -ing form and cannot start an imperative.

hints: Imperatives use the base form of the verb without any ending.
'Before dinner' provides the time context — this is an instruction to do homework.

a4.Do: Correct: 'Do' is the base form. 'Do your homework' is a standard instruction.
a4.Does: Wrong: 'Does' is the third-person form. Imperatives always use the base form.
a4.Doing: Wrong: 'Doing' is the -ing form, unsuitable for starting a command.

hints: The imperative = infinitive without 'to': look, not looks or looking.
The preposition 'at' after the gap forms the phrasal verb 'look at' (to look at something).

a5.Look: Correct: 'Look at the picture' — base form imperative.
a5.Looks: Wrong: 'Looks' has -s for third person. Imperatives have no -s.
a5.Looking: Wrong: 'Looking' is the -ing form. Commands use the base form.

hints: Commands use the base form with no endings at all.
'Your name on the paper' after the gap shows this is an instruction to write.

a6.Write: Correct: 'Write your name' — base form imperative.
a6.Writes: Wrong: 'Writes' is third-person singular. Imperatives use the base form only.
a6.Writing: Wrong: 'Writing' is the -ing form, not used to begin commands.

hints: Negative imperatives are formed with Don't + base form.
After the gap, the base form 'run' completes the negative command.

a7.Doesn't: Wrong: 'Doesn't' is for third-person negation. Imperatives use 'Don't'.
a7.Not: Wrong: 'Not run' is not a grammatical imperative. Use 'Don't' before the verb.

hints: Negative commands: Don't + base form. Neither 'Doesn't' nor 'Not' works here.
The exclamation mark emphasises the warning — this is a negative command.

a8.Don't: Correct: 'Don't touch' — negative imperative with a warning tone.
a8.Doesn't: Wrong: 'Doesn't' is third-person negation, not used in imperatives.
a8.Not: Wrong: 'Not touch' is ungrammatical. Negative imperatives require 'Don't'.

hints: The negative imperative formula is always: Don't + base form.
After the gap, 'eat' is the base form — this is a classroom rule.

a9.Doesn't: Wrong: 'Doesn't eat' is Present Simple for he/she, not a command.
a9.Not: Wrong: 'Not eat' is ungrammatical for a negative imperative.

hints: After 'Please', a negative imperative still uses don't + base form.
The base form 'talk' after the gap and the context 'during the film' explain the prohibition.

a10.don't: Correct: 'Please don't talk' — polite negative imperative.
a10.doesn't: Wrong: 'doesn't' is third-person. Even with 'Please', use 'don't' for commands.
a10.not: Wrong: 'Please not talk' is ungrammatical. Use 'Please don't talk'.

hints: The negative imperative of 'be' is: Don't be + adjective. 'Not be' and 'Doesn't be' don't exist.
After the gap, 'late for school' shows this is a command not to be late.

a11.Don't be: Correct: 'Don't be late' — standard negative imperative with 'be'.
a11.Not be: Wrong: 'Not be late' is ungrammatical. English requires 'Don't be' for negative imperatives.
a11.Doesn't be: Wrong: 'Doesn't be' does not exist in English. Use 'Don't be'.

hints: Commands start with the base form. 'Turn off' is a phrasal verb meaning 'to switch off'.
After the gap, 'off the lights' completes the phrasal verb instruction.

a12.Turn: Correct: 'Turn off' — base form of the phrasal verb in a command.
a12.Turns: Wrong: 'Turns' is the third-person form with -s. Commands use the base form.
a12.Turning: Wrong: 'Turning' is the -ing form, unsuitable for a command.

hints: The imperative of 'listen' is simply 'Listen' — no endings.
The preposition 'to' after the gap indicates 'listen to' (the audio).

a13.Listen: Correct: 'Listen to the audio' — base form imperative.
a13.Listens: Wrong: 'Listens' is third person. Commands use the base form.
a13.Listening: Wrong: 'Listening' is the -ing form, not used for commands.

hints: The base form 'Read' is used for commands — it looks the same as the infinitive.
After the gap, 'the text on the board' is the object of the command.

a14.Read: Correct: 'Read the text' — base form imperative.
a14.Reads: Wrong: 'Reads' has -s for he/she. Commands use the base form.
a14.Reading: Wrong: 'Reading' is the -ing form, not suitable for an imperative.

hints: The imperative of 'be' is 'Be' (not 'Are' or 'Being').
After the gap, the adjective 'quiet' and reason 'the baby is sleeping' indicate a command to be quiet.

a15.Are: Wrong: 'Are' is for statements (You are quiet), not commands.
a15.Being: Wrong: 'Being quiet' is an -ing construction, not an imperative.

hints: Commands use the base form: Shut, not Shuts or Shutting.
After the gap, 'the door behind you' is a common instruction.

a16.Shut: Correct: 'Shut the door' — base form imperative.
a16.Shuts: Wrong: 'Shuts' has -s for third person. Commands use the base form.
a16.Shutting: Wrong: 'Shutting' is the -ing form, not used for commands.

hints: The imperative of 'be' is always 'Be', regardless of the implied subject.
After the gap, 'careful' is an adjective — 'Be careful' is advice.

a17.Are: Wrong: 'Are careful' is a statement (You are careful), not a command.
a17.Is: Wrong: 'Is' is for third-person singular, not used in imperatives.

hints: Imperatives start with the base form: Raise, not Raises or Raising.
'Your hand' after the gap is a typical classroom instruction.

a18.Raise: Correct: 'Raise your hand' — base form imperative.
a18.Raises: Wrong: 'Raises' has -s for he/she. Commands use the base form.
a18.Raising: Wrong: 'Raising' is the -ing form, not suitable for commands.

hints: After 'Please', the verb stays in base form: pass, not passes.
The pronoun 'me' and 'the salt' after the gap indicate a polite request.

a19.pass: Correct: 'Please pass me' — polite imperative with base form.
a19.passes: Wrong: 'passes' is third-person. Even with 'please', use the base form.
a19.passing: Wrong: 'passing' is the -ing form, not suitable for an imperative.

hints: Imperative with object pronoun: Tell + him/her/them. Base form.
'Him about the meeting' after the gap is the instruction content.

a20.Tell: Correct: 'Tell him' — base form + object pronoun in an imperative.
a20.Tells: Wrong: 'Tells' has -s. Commands always use the base form.
a20.Telling: Wrong: 'Telling' is the -ing form, not used for commands.

hints: The verb at the start of an imperative stays in base form, even with 'please' at the end.
'Her the message, please' after the gap is a polite request.

a21.Give: Correct: 'Give her the message' — base form with two objects.
a21.Gives: Wrong: 'Gives' is third-person with -s. Commands don't use -s.
a21.Giving: Wrong: 'Giving' is the -ing form, not for imperatives.

hints: After 'Please', 'be' keeps its base form — not 'are' or 'being'.
'Quiet in the library' after the gap is the context for a polite request.

a22.be: Correct: 'Please be quiet' — polite imperative with 'be' + adjective.
a22.are: Wrong: 'are' is for statements (you are quiet), not for imperatives.
a22.being: Wrong: 'being' is the -ing form and cannot form an imperative.

hints: Imperative with object pronoun 'them': Ask + them + to-infinitive.
'Them to come inside' shows the instruction content.

a23.Ask: Correct: 'Ask them' — base form + object pronoun.
a23.Asks: Wrong: 'Asks' has -s for he/she. Commands use the base form.
a23.Asking: Wrong: 'Asking' is the -ing form, not for imperatives.

hints: After 'Always', the verb in an imperative remains in base form.
'Always' intensifies a habitual instruction; 'before bed' gives the time context.

a24.brush: Correct: 'Always brush' — imperative with 'always' + base form.
a24.brushes: Wrong: 'brushes' is third-person. Even with 'always', commands use the base form.
a24.brushing: Wrong: 'brushing' is the -ing form, not for imperatives after 'always'.

hints: After 'Never', the verb stays in base form: talk, not talks.
'Never' replaces 'Don't ever' and creates a strong negative command.

a25.talk: Correct: 'Never talk' — negative imperative with 'Never' + base form.
a25.talks: Wrong: 'talks' is third-person. After 'Never' in commands, use the base form.
a25.talking: Wrong: 'talking' is the -ing form, not used after 'Never' in imperatives.

hints: Imperative with object pronoun 'it': Put + it + preposition + place.
'Please' at the end makes the command polite but the verb stays in base form.

a26.Put: Correct: 'Put it on the shelf' — base form with object pronoun.
a26.Puts: Wrong: 'Puts' is third-person with -s. Commands use the base form.
a26.Putting: Wrong: 'Putting' is the -ing form, not for imperatives.

hints: Imperative with two objects: Tell + me + the truth. Base form.
'The truth' after the gap is the direct object of the command.

a27.Tell: Correct: 'Tell me the truth' — base form imperative with object pronoun.
a27.Tells: Wrong: 'Tells' has -s. Commands use the base form.
a27.Told: Wrong: 'Told' is past tense. Imperatives always use the base form.

hints: After 'Please', the verb stays in base form: tell, not tells or told.
The pronoun 'us' and 'a story' indicate a polite request.

a28.tell: Correct: 'Please tell us' — polite imperative with base form.
a28.tells: Wrong: 'tells' is third-person. Polite imperatives still use the base form.
a28.told: Wrong: 'told' is past tense. Imperatives use the base form only.

hints: 'Make yourself at home' is a fixed expression using the imperative.
The reflexive pronoun 'yourself' after the gap is part of this hospitality formula.

a29.Make: Correct: 'Make yourself at home' — fixed imperative expression.
a29.Makes: Wrong: 'Makes' is third-person. Commands use the base form.
a29.Making: Wrong: 'Making' is the -ing form, not suitable for this fixed expression.

hints: After 'Please', use the base form: leave, not leaves or leaving.
'Me alone' indicates a polite request to be left alone.

a30.leave: Correct: 'Please leave me alone' — polite imperative.
a30.leaves: Wrong: 'leaves' is third-person. Even with 'please', use the base form.
a30.leaving: Wrong: 'leaving' is the -ing form, not for imperatives.

hints: After 'Always' in an imperative, the verb stays in base form.
'After receiving a gift' provides the context for the advice.

a31.say: Correct: 'Always say' — imperative advice with base form.
a31.says: Wrong: 'says' is third-person. Commands after 'always' use the base form.
a31.said: Wrong: 'said' is past tense. Imperatives use the base form.

hints: After 'Never', the verb is in base form: throw, not throws or threw.
'Never' creates a strong negative command without 'Don't'.

a32.throw: Correct: 'Never throw' — negative command with base form.
a32.throws: Wrong: 'throws' is third-person. After 'Never', use the base form.
a32.threw: Wrong: 'threw' is past tense. Imperatives always use the base form.

hints: Commands start with the base form: Hold, not Holds or Holding.
'The baby carefully' is the instruction content after the gap.

a33.Hold: Correct: 'Hold the baby carefully' — base form imperative.
a33.Holds: Wrong: 'Holds' has -s. Commands use the base form.
a33.Holding: Wrong: 'Holding' is the -ing form, not for commands.

hints: After 'Please', the verb stays in base form: call, not calls or called.
'Me when you arrive' gives the context of the polite request.

a34.call: Correct: 'Please call me' — polite imperative with base form.
a34.calls: Wrong: 'calls' is third-person. Polite imperatives use the base form.
a34.called: Wrong: 'called' is past tense. Imperatives use the base form.

hints: After 'Always', the imperative uses the base form: look, not looks.
'Both ways' after the gap indicates the safety advice context.

a35.look: Correct: 'Always look both ways' — imperative advice with base form.
a35.looks: Wrong: 'looks' is third-person. After 'always', commands use the base form.
a35.looking: Wrong: 'looking' is the -ing form, not for imperatives.

hints: Imperative with object pronoun: Tell + them. Base form.
The exclamation mark and 'right away' add urgency to the command.

a36.Tell: Correct: 'Tell them' — base form imperative with object pronoun.
a36.Tells: Wrong: 'Tells' has -s. Commands use the base form.
a36.Telling: Wrong: 'Telling' is the -ing form, not for commands.

hints: Suggestions for shared action use: Let's (= Let us) + base form.
After the gap, 'go to the cinema' is the suggested activity.

a37.Let's: Correct: 'Let's go' — standard suggestion. The apostrophe is required: Let's = Let us.
a37.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets' without an apostrophe is the third-person verb (he lets). Suggestions need 'Let's'.
a37.Let: Wrong: 'Let go' without 'us' means to release. Suggestions need 'Let's'.

hints: Let's + base form = a suggestion to do something together.
'Try the new Italian restaurant' is the shared suggestion.

a38.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets' is third-person (he lets). Use 'Let's' for suggestions.
a38.Let: Wrong: 'Let try' is incomplete. Use 'Let's' (= Let us).

hints: Negative suggestion: Let's not + base form. The apostrophe is required.
'Not argue' after the gap forms the negative suggestion.

a39.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets not' is third person without apostrophe.
a39.Let: Wrong: 'Let not argue' is incomplete. Use 'Let's not'.

hints: Let + me/him/her/them + base form = request for permission or an offer to help.
'Me help you' after the gap is an offer. 'Let' without an apostrophe.

a40.Let: Correct: 'Let me help' — offer/request. 'Let' + object pronoun.
a40.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets me' is third-person Present Simple, not an imperative.
a40.Let's: Wrong: 'Let's me help' — 'Let's' already contains 'us', so adding 'me' is ungrammatical.

hints: Let + him + base form = permission or instruction regarding a third person.
'Him finish his speech' is a request to allow him to finish.

a41.Let: Correct: 'Let him finish' — giving permission. 'Let' + object pronoun.
a41.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets him' is third-person (she lets him). The imperative is just 'Let'.
a41.Let's: Wrong: 'Let's him' — 'Let's' = 'Let us'; adding 'him' is ungrammatical.

hints: Let + her + base form: allow her to do something.
'Her decide on her own' is the instruction not to interfere.

a42.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets her' is third-person. The imperative is 'Let'.
a42.Let's: Wrong: 'Let's her decide' — 'Let's' = 'Let us'; 'her' after it is ungrammatical.

hints: Emphatic imperative: Do + base form. 'Do' adds emphasis or politeness.
The dash and 'you must be very tired' explain the emphatic invitation to sit down.

a43.Do: Correct: 'Do sit down' — emphatic imperative. 'Do' adds warmth/insistence.
a43.Does: Wrong: 'Does' is third-person. Emphatic imperatives use 'Do' only.
a43.Did: Wrong: 'Did' is past tense. Emphatic imperatives use 'Do'.

hints: Do + be + adjective = emphatic imperative with 'be'.
The context 'icy roads' makes the warning serious; 'Do' emphasises it.

a44.Do: Correct: 'Do be careful' — emphatic imperative with 'be'.
a44.Does: Wrong: 'Does be' is ungrammatical. Use 'Do' for emphasis.
a44.Did: Wrong: 'Did be' is ungrammatical. Emphatic imperatives use 'Do'.

hints: Do + base form = emphatic request, adding sincerity or politeness.
'Forgive me' after the gap is a sincere, emphasised apology.

a45.Do: Correct: 'Do forgive me' — emphatic polite request.
a45.Does: Wrong: 'Does' is third-person. Emphatic imperatives use 'Do'.
a45.Did: Wrong: 'Did' is past tense. Emphatic imperatives are always 'Do'.

hints: Negative imperative: Don't + base form.
After the gap, 'waste time' is the base form; 'let's start' contrasts with the prohibition.

a46.Don't: Correct: 'Don't waste time' — standard negative imperative.
a46.Doesn't: Wrong: 'Doesn't' is third-person negation. Imperatives use 'Don't'.
a46.Didn't: Wrong: 'Didn't' is past tense negation. Imperatives use 'Don't'.

hints: Don't + base form (let) = negative command.
'Let the opportunity slip away' is the action being prohibited.

a47.Doesn't: Wrong: 'Doesn't let' is third-person, not an imperative.
a47.Didn't: Wrong: 'Didn't let' is past tense, not an imperative.

hints: Don't ever = Never — this is an intensified negative command.
'Give up' after the gap completes the motivational command.

a48.Don't: Correct: 'Don't ever give up' — intensified negative imperative.
a48.Didn't: Wrong: 'Didn't' is past tense. Imperatives use 'Don't'.

hints: Let's + base form = suggestion for a shared action.
'Take a short break' is the suggestion.

a49.Let's: Correct: 'Let's take a break' — shared suggestion.
a49.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets' without apostrophe is third person.

hints: Let + me + know: a standard formula meaning 'inform me'. No apostrophe.
'Me know if you need anything' is the request content.

a50.Let: Correct: 'Let me know' — standard request formula.
a50.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets me' is third-person (she lets me). The imperative is 'Let'.
a50.Let's: Wrong: 'Let's me know' — 'Let's' = 'Let us'; 'me' after it is redundant.

hints: Do + base form = emphatic invitation.
'The door is open' after the dash reinforces the welcoming tone.

a51.Does: Wrong: 'Does come' is third-person. Emphatic imperatives use 'Do'.
a51.Did: Wrong: 'Did come' is past tense. Emphatic imperatives use 'Do'.

hints: Let + them + base form: permission for third persons.
'Them play outside' gives permission for children to play.

a52.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets them' is third-person. The imperative is 'Let'.
a52.Let's: Wrong: 'Let's them' — 'Let's' = 'Let us'; 'them' is ungrammatical after it.

hints: Let + me + base form: request for time or permission.
'Me think about it' is a request for a moment to think.

a53.Let: Correct: 'Let me think' — request for time. No apostrophe.
a53.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets me' is third-person. The imperative is 'Let'.
a53.Let's: Wrong: 'Let's me' — 'Let's' already has 'us'; 'me' is redundant.

hints: Negative suggestion: Let's not + base form.
'Not forget to lock the front door' is the reminder.

a54.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets not' lacks the apostrophe needed for a suggestion.
a54.Let: Wrong: 'Let not forget' is incomplete. Use 'Let's not'.

hints: Passive imperative: Be + past participle. 'Be prepared' = be ready.
'At all times' emphasises the ongoing nature of the instruction.

a55.Be: Correct: 'Be prepared' — passive imperative. 'Be' + past participle.
a55.Being: Wrong: 'Being prepared' is an -ing form, not an imperative.
a55.Been: Wrong: 'Been prepared' is a Perfect form, not an imperative.

hints: Negative passive imperative: Be not + past participle (formal) or Don't be + past participle.
'Initial failures' provides the context for the encouraging advice.

a56.Be: Correct: 'Be not discouraged' — formal negative passive imperative.
a56.Being: Wrong: 'Being not discouraged' is an -ing form, not an imperative.
a56.Been: Wrong: 'Been not discouraged' is a Perfect form, not an imperative.

hints: 'Be warned' is a passive imperative meaning 'consider yourself warned'.
The dash and 'the road ahead is very steep' explain the warning.

a57.Being: Wrong: 'Being warned' is -ing, not an imperative.
a57.Been: Wrong: 'Been warned' is Perfect, not an imperative.

hints: 'Rest assured' is a fixed imperative expression meaning 'be confident'.
'That we will resolve this' follows the reassurance.

a58.Rests: Wrong: 'Rests' is third-person. The imperative is the base form.
a58.Resting: Wrong: 'Resting' is -ing, unsuitable for this fixed expression.

hints: 'Remain seated' is a formal imperative, common in aviation.
'Until the aircraft comes to a complete stop' is the standard safety instruction.
'Be advised' is a formal passive imperative, common in official notices.
After 'Be advised', a 'that' clause provides the information.

a60.Been: Wrong: 'Been advised' is Perfect, not imperative.

hints: Question tags for imperatives use 'will you?' or 'won't you?'.
After the comma, a question tag softens the command.

a61.will you: Correct: 'will you?' — standard question tag for imperatives.
a61.do you: Wrong: 'do you?' is for Present Simple statements, not imperatives.
a61.are you: Wrong: 'are you?' is for 'be' statements, not imperative tags.

hints: The question tag for imperatives is 'will you?' — it softens the request.
The base form 'Pass' confirms this is an imperative sentence.

a62.will you: Correct: 'will you?' — polite tag for imperative.
a62.do you: Wrong: 'do you?' is not the correct tag for imperatives.
a62.are you: Wrong: 'are you?' is not used as a tag for imperatives.

hints: After negative imperatives (Don't...), the question tag is also 'will you?'.
The imperative 'Don't forget' takes the same tag as affirmative imperatives.

a63.will you: Correct: 'will you?' — tag for both affirmative and negative imperatives.
a63.do you: Wrong: 'do you?' is not for imperative tags.
a63.are you: Wrong: 'are you?' is not for imperative tags.

hints: The question tag for 'Let's...' is always 'shall we?' — not 'will we?' or 'do we?'.
'Let's go' is a suggestion; 'shall we?' asks for agreement.

a64.shall we: Correct: 'shall we?' — the only correct tag for 'Let's' suggestions.
a64.will we: Wrong: 'will we?' is not used after 'Let's'. Use 'shall we?'.
a64.do we: Wrong: 'do we?' is not a valid tag for 'Let's' sentences.

hints: Negative imperative 'Don't...' takes the question tag 'will you?'.
The command 'Don't make noise' is softened by the tag.

a65.will you: Correct: 'will you?' — tag for negative imperatives.
a65.do you: Wrong: 'do you?' is not for imperative tags.
a65.shall you: Wrong: 'shall you?' is not a valid imperative tag combination.

hints: For polite invitations, 'won't you?' is the tag — it sounds hospitable.
'Have a seat' is a polite invitation; 'won't you?' adds warmth.

a66.won't you: Correct: 'won't you?' — hospitable tag for polite invitations.
a66.don't you: Wrong: 'don't you?' is for statements, not imperative invitations.
a66.aren't you: Wrong: 'aren't you?' is not used as a tag for imperatives.

hints: In a coordinated command: 'Finish..., and then [command]' — the second part is also a base form.
After 'and then', the base form 'give' completes the second command.

a67.give: Correct: 'give yourself a break' — second command in coordination.
a67.gives: Wrong: 'gives' is third-person. Both commands must be in base form.
a67.giving: Wrong: 'giving' is -ing, not suitable for a command.

hints: Imperative in the main clause after 'If...': base form verb.
After the condition 'If you see Sarah', the command follows in base form.

a68.tell: Correct: 'tell her' — base form imperative after a conditional clause.
a68.tells: Wrong: 'tells' is third-person. After a condition, the command is base form.
a68.telling: Wrong: 'telling' is -ing, not a valid main clause command.

hints: After formal inverted conditionals (Should + subject + verb), the main clause uses base form imperative.
'Should the alarm go off' is the inverted conditional; then comes the command.

a69.evacuate: Correct: 'evacuate the building' — imperative after formal condition.
a69.evacuates: Wrong: 'evacuates' is third-person. Commands use the base form.

hints: 'As prescribed' is a reduced passive clause (= as it was prescribed). Past participle needed.
After 'as', the past participle gives the passive meaning 'as ordered by the doctor'.

a70.prescribed: Correct: 'as prescribed' — past participle in a reduced passive.
a70.prescribing: Wrong: 'as prescribing' is -ing, not the correct passive form.
a70.prescribe: Wrong: 'as prescribe' is the base form, but passive requires past participle.

hints: 'When in Rome, do as the Romans do' is a proverb. The command uses base form 'do'.
The imperative part of this proverb is 'do' in base form.

a71.do: Correct: 'do as the Romans do' — base form in a proverbial command.
a71.does: Wrong: 'does' is third-person. The proverb uses the base form imperative.
a71.doing: Wrong: 'doing' is -ing. The proverb uses the base form.

hints: After 'In case of fire', a safety instruction follows in base form.
This is a standard safety instruction; the command uses the base form.

a72.use: Correct: 'use the stairs' — base form imperative in a safety instruction.
a72.uses: Wrong: 'uses' is third-person. Instructions use the base form.
a72.using: Wrong: 'using' is -ing, not suitable for a direct instruction.

hints: 'Do under no circumstances' = emphatic imperative with a formal prohibition.
'Under no circumstances' is a formal marker of categorical prohibition.

a73.Do: Correct: 'Do under no circumstances reveal' — emphatic formal prohibition.
a73.Does: Wrong: 'Does' is third-person. Emphatic imperatives use 'Do'.
a73.Did: Wrong: 'Did' is past tense. Emphatic imperatives use 'Do'.

hints: 'Let me rephrase' = formal request. 'Let' without apostrophe + 'me'.
The colon separates the request from the rephrased statement.

a74.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets me' is third-person. The imperative is 'Let'.
a74.Let's: Wrong: 'Let's me' — 'Let's' = 'Let us'; adding 'me' is ungrammatical.

hints: 'Let it be known' = formal declaration. 'Let' + 'it' + 'be' + past participle.
This is a standard phrase for official announcements.

a75.Let: Correct: 'Let it be known' — formal declaration.
a75.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets it' is third-person. Formal commands use 'Let'.
a75.Let's: Wrong: 'Let's it' — 'Let's' = 'Let us'; 'it' does not fit after 'Let's'.

hints: After 'Make no mistake —' a second command follows in base form.
The dash separates the warning from the follow-up command.

a76.take: Correct: 'take this opportunity' — base form command after the dash.
a76.takes: Wrong: 'takes' is third-person. Coordinated commands use base form.
a76.taking: Wrong: 'taking' is -ing, not a valid command.

hints: 'On no account' is a formal negative marker. The verb after it is in base form.
This phrase replaces 'Don't ever' in formal style.

a77.block: Correct: 'On no account block' — formal prohibition with base form.
a77.blocks: Wrong: 'blocks' is third-person. Formal commands use the base form.
a77.blocking: Wrong: 'blocking' is -ing, not suitable for formal prohibitions.

hints: 'Under no circumstances' = categorical formal prohibition. Base form follows.
Information security context emphasises the seriousness of the prohibition.

a78.store: Correct: 'Under no circumstances store' — formal prohibition.
a78.stores: Wrong: 'stores' is third-person. Formal commands use the base form.
a78.storing: Wrong: 'storing' is -ing, not for formal prohibitions.

hints: 'Follow these instructions' — formal command. Base form at the start.
'To the letter' means 'exactly' and emphasises precision.

a79.Follow: Correct: 'Follow these instructions' — formal command in base form.
a79.Follows: Wrong: 'Follows' is third-person. Formal commands use the base form.
a79.Following: Wrong: 'Following' is -ing, not a direct command.

hints: 'Take note' is a formal command meaning 'pay attention'. Base form.
After 'Take note that', a subordinate clause provides the information.

a80.Take: Correct: 'Take note' — formal command in base form.
a80.Takes: Wrong: 'Takes' is third-person. Commands use the base form.

hints: 'Remove' — a formal instruction in base form.
The context 'from the conference room' confirms the formal instruction.

a81.Remove: Correct: 'Remove all personal belongings' — formal instruction.

hints: After 'Bear in mind that', the subordinate clause has 'punctuality' as subject — third-person singular.
In the subordinate clause, the verb agrees with 'punctuality' (singular): reflects.

a82.reflects: Correct: 'punctuality reflects' — third-person singular in the subordinate clause.
a82.reflect: Wrong: 'reflect' lacks -s. 'Punctuality' is singular and needs -s.
a82.reflecting: Wrong: 'reflecting' is -ing. The subordinate clause needs Present Simple with -s.

hints: 'Consider this memo' — formal command. Base form at the start.
'Consider... as' means 'regard as' in a formal context.

a83.Considers: Wrong: 'Considers' is third-person. Formal commands use the base form.
a83.Considering: Wrong: 'Considering' is -ing or a preposition, not a command.

hints: 'Be hereby informed' — formal passive imperative. 'Be' in base form.
'Hereby' (by this means) is typical of legal and official documents.

a84.Be: Correct: 'Be hereby informed' — formal passive imperative.
a84.Been: Wrong: 'Been' is Perfect form, not an imperative.

hints: After 'Kindly' (a formal politeness marker), the verb is in base form.
'Kindly refrain from' is a standard formal polite request.

a85.refrain: Correct: 'Kindly refrain from' — formal polite imperative.
a85.refrains: Wrong: 'refrains' is third-person. Even with 'Kindly', use the base form.

hints: 'Refer to' — formal command meaning 'consult'. Base form.
'Appendix B' context indicates a formal document reference.

a86.Refer: Correct: 'Refer to the guidelines' — formal command.
a86.Refers: Wrong: 'Refers' is third-person. Commands use the base form.

hints: 'Direct all complaints' — formal command in base form.
'Through the proper channel' confirms the official instruction.

a87.Directs: Wrong: 'Directs' is third-person. Commands use the base form.

hints: 'Do not hesitate' — formal negative imperative. After 'Do', the negation is 'not'.
In formal style, 'Do not' is used instead of the contraction 'Don't'.

a88.not: Correct: 'Do not hesitate' — formal negation. 'Not' goes between 'Do' and the verb.
a88.no: Wrong: 'Do no hesitate' is ungrammatical. Use 'not' between 'Do' and the verb.
a88.never: Wrong: 'Do never hesitate' is ungrammatical. 'Never' doesn't go between 'Do' and verb.

hints: 'Keep in mind' — formal command, synonym of 'Bear in mind'. Base form.
After 'Keep in mind that', a subordinate clause provides key information.

a89.Keep: Correct: 'Keep in mind' — formal command in base form.
a89.Keeps: Wrong: 'Keeps' is third-person. Commands use the base form.

hints: After 'Never' in a formal command, the verb stays in base form: send.
'Unencrypted email' context emphasises the security prohibition.

a90.send: Correct: 'Never send' — formal prohibition with base form.
a90.sends: Wrong: 'sends' is third-person. After 'Never', use the base form.
a90.sending: Wrong: 'sending' is -ing, not suitable after 'Never' in commands.

hints: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed subjunctive expression meaning 'however that may be'.
'Be' is the subjunctive form (= base form of 'be') in this literary formula.

a91.Be: Correct: 'Be that as it may' — subjunctive formula with base form 'Be'.
a91.Being: Wrong: 'Being' is -ing, not used in this fixed expression.
a91.Is: Wrong: 'Is' is indicative. This expression requires the subjunctive 'Be'.

hints: 'Let there be light' — a biblical creation phrase. 'Let' without apostrophe.
After the gap, 'there be light' uses 'Let' + 'there be' + noun.

a92.Let: Correct: 'Let there be light' — classic creation imperative.
a92.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets there be' is third-person. The imperative is 'Let'.
a92.Let's: Wrong: 'Let's there be' — 'Let's' = 'Let us'; incompatible with 'there be'.

hints: 'Let the record show' — a formal legal formula. 'Let' + subject + base form.
Common in court proceedings and official meetings.

a93.Let: Correct: 'Let the record show' — formal legal imperative.
a93.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets the record' is third-person. Formal imperative is 'Let'.
a93.Let's: Wrong: 'Let's the record' — 'Let's' = 'Let us'; doesn't work with 'the record' as subject.

hints: 'Take heed' — literary/archaic command meaning 'be careful'. Base form.
'Lest' (= so that... not) is typical of formal/literary style.

a94.Take: Correct: 'Take heed' — literary command in base form.
a94.Takes: Wrong: 'Takes' is third-person. Even in literary style, imperatives use base form.
a94.Taking: Wrong: 'Taking' is -ing, not a literary imperative.

hints: 'Perish the thought' is a fixed expression meaning 'God forbid'. The noun 'thought' is needed.
After the article 'the', a noun is required — 'thought', not a verb form.

a95.thought: Correct: 'Perish the thought' — fixed expression with the noun 'thought'.
a95.think: Wrong: 'the think' is ungrammatical. After 'the', a noun is needed: 'thought'.
a95.thinking: Wrong: Although 'thinking' can be a noun, the fixed expression requires 'thought'.

hints: 'Let it never be said' — literary formula with 'Let' + 'it' + 'never' + 'be said'.
'Let' without apostrophe, because 'it' follows, not 'us'.

a96.Let: Correct: 'Let it never be said' — literary imperative.
a96.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets it' is third-person. The imperative is 'Let'.
a96.Let's: Wrong: 'Let's it' — 'Let's' = 'Let us'; 'it' after it is ungrammatical.

hints: 'Waste not, want not' is a proverb. 'Want' here means 'lack' (archaic).
Both parts are imperatives in base form.

a97.want: Correct: 'want not' — base form in the proverb. 'Want' = to lack (archaic).
a97.wants: Wrong: 'wants not' is third-person. The proverb uses imperative base forms.
a97.wanting: Wrong: 'wanting not' is -ing. The proverb uses imperative base forms.

hints: 'Far be it from me' — fixed subjunctive expression meaning 'it is not for me to...'.
After 'Far', the subjunctive 'be' (not 'is' or 'was') is required.

a98.be: Correct: 'Far be it from me' — subjunctive fixed expression.
a98.is: Wrong: 'Far is it from me' breaks the fixed expression. Subjunctive 'be' is required.
a98.was: Wrong: 'Far was it' is past tense. The expression uses subjunctive 'be'.

hints: This is from Shakespeare's Sonnet 116. 'Let me not' = imperative with 'Let' + 'me' + 'not'.
'Let' without apostrophe + object pronoun 'me' + negation 'not'.

a99.Let: Correct: 'Let me not' — literary imperative from Shakespeare.
a99.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets me' is third-person. The literary imperative is 'Let'.
a99.Let's: Wrong: 'Let's me not' — 'Let's' = 'Let us'; 'me' after it is ungrammatical.

hints: 'Let no one say' — formal imperative meaning 'may nobody say'. 'Let' without apostrophe.
'No one' is the subject after 'Let', followed by the base form 'say'.

a100.Let: Correct: 'Let no one say' — formal imperative.
a100.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets no one' is third-person. The formal imperative is 'Let'.
a100.Let's: Wrong: 'Let's no one' — 'Let's' = 'Let us'; 'no one' as subject doesn't fit.

hints: 'Come what may' — fixed subjunctive expression meaning 'whatever happens'.
'Come' is in the subjunctive/imperative base form in this fixed phrase.

a101.Comes: Wrong: 'Comes what may' breaks the fixed expression. Base form 'Come' is required.
a101.Coming: Wrong: 'Coming what may' is -ing, incompatible with the fixed expression.

hints: 'Suffice it to say' — formal expression meaning 'it is enough to say'. After 'to', base form.
The infinitive 'to say' is the standard part of this fixed expression.

a102.say: Correct: 'Suffice it to say' — base form after 'to'.
a102.says: Wrong: 'to says' — after 'to', -s is never added.
a102.said: Wrong: 'to said' — after 'to', past tense is never used.

hints: Two coordinated imperatives joined by 'and': 'Hear me and understand'. Both base form.
After 'and', the second verb is also in base form.

a103.understand: Correct: 'Hear me and understand' — coordinated imperatives.
a103.understands: Wrong: 'understands' is third-person. Both commands need base form.
a103.understanding: Wrong: 'understanding' is -ing. The second command also needs base form.

hints: 'Dare not' — archaic/literary negative construction. After 'Dare', use 'not'.
'Dare' in this imperative is negated by 'not', not by 'no' or 'never'.

a104.not: Correct: 'Dare not defy' — archaic negation. 'Dare not' = do not dare.
a104.no: Wrong: 'Dare no defy' is ungrammatical. Use 'not' after 'Dare'.
a104.never: Wrong: 'Dare never defy' — while semantically possible, the fixed construction uses 'not'.

hints: 'Examine your conscience' — literary command in base form.
'And' connects two coordinated commands: 'Examine' and 'act'.

a105.Examines: Wrong: 'Examines' is third-person. Commands use the base form.
a105.Examining: Wrong: 'Examining' is -ing, not a literary imperative.

hints: 'Be it resolved' — parliamentary/legal formula. 'Be' in subjunctive/imperative form.
This is the standard beginning of formal resolutions.

a106.Be: Correct: 'Be it resolved' — legal formula with subjunctive 'Be'.
a106.Being: Wrong: 'Being it resolved' breaks the legal formula.
a106.Been: Wrong: 'Been it resolved' is Perfect form, not the formula.

hints: 'Heaven forbid' — fixed subjunctive expression meaning 'God forbid'. Base form after 'Heaven'.
'Heaven' + subjunctive 'forbid' (not 'forbids') is the fixed formula.

a107.forbid: Correct: 'Heaven forbid' — subjunctive. Base form despite singular subject.
a107.forbids: Wrong: 'Heaven forbids' — the fixed expression uses subjunctive 'forbid' without -s.
a107.forbidden: Wrong: 'Heaven forbidden' — past participle doesn't fit this expression.

hints: 'Let me be frank' — formal request to speak openly. 'Let' + 'me' + 'be' + adjective.
The dash separates the request from the subsequent statement.

a108.Let: Correct: 'Let me be frank' — formal request.
a108.Lets: Wrong: 'Lets me' is third-person. The imperative is 'Let'.
a108.Let's: Wrong: 'Let's me' — 'Let's' = 'Let us'; 'me' after it is redundant.
